elements.sent-2411-001: In this work, we study the gradient discretisation method (GDM) of the time-dependent Navier-Stokes equations coupled with the heat equation, where the viscosity depends on the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-002: We design the discrete method and prove its convergence without non-physical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-003: The paper is closed with numerical experiments that confirm the theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-004: Navier-Stokes problems are of significant importance for a variety of applications.
elements.sent-2411-005: Examples are cooling processes, industrial furnaces, boilers, and heat exchangers \cite{B2 .}
elements.sent-2411-006: In this work, we extend the gradient discretisation method (GDM) to the transient coupled Navier-Stokes and heat equations describing the flow of a viscous incompressible fluid.
elements.sent-2411-007: The GDM introduced in \cite{B1 is an abstract setting that covers different families of numerical schemes, and it has been applied successfully for various problems in porous media.}
elements.sent-2411-008:
The model we consider here is governed by the unknowns $ ar \u$, $ ar p$, and $ ar S$ satisfying the following equations:  egin{subequations  egin{equation}\dr_t  ar\u-\div(V( ar S)
abla ar \u)+( ar \u\cdot 
abla ar \u)+
abla ar p=\g \quad in $\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-1} \div  ar \u=0 \quad in $\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-2} \dr_t  ar S-\mu\Delta ar S + ( ar \u\cdot
abla) ar S=h \quad in $\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-3}  ar \u=0 \quad on $\dr\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-4}  ar S=0 \quad on $\dr\O 	imes (0,T)$\label{nsp-5},  ar\u(\x,0)=\u_0 \quad in $\O$,\label{nsp-5}  ar S(\x,0)=S_0 \quad in $\O$.\label{nsp-6}\end{equation} \end{subequations Here, the vector $ ar \u$ and the variables $ ar p$ and $ ar S$ denote the velocity, the pressure and the temperature of the fluid, respectively, and they are defined on the polygonal domain $\O\subset \RR^d$ $(d=2,3)$.}}

abla ar \u)+( ar \u\cdot

abla ar p=\g \quad in $\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-1} \div  ar \u=0 \quad in $\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-2} \dr_t  ar S-\mu\Delta ar S + ( ar \u\cdot
abla) ar S=h \quad in $\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-3}  ar \u=0 \quad on $\dr\O 	imes (0,T)$, \label{nsp-4}  ar S=0 \quad on $\dr\O 	imes (0,T)$\label{nsp-5},  ar\u(\x,0)=\u_0 \quad in $\O$,\label{nsp-5}  ar S(\x,0)=S_0 \quad in $\O$.\label{nsp-6}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-009: The functions $\g$ and $h$ denote the distributions, and $\u_0$ and $S_0$ denote the initial data and temperature inside the body $\O$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Note that the model involves non-linear viscosity ($V$) depending on the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-011: We focus here on homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions for simplicity, whereas our analysis can easily be extended to different conditions.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is proved in \cite{REF1 that the model \eqref{nsp has a weak solution under standard assumptions on data.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Because of the non-linearity term, it is not directly used to compute analytical solutions.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, there is a high demand for numerical approximation to find a solution in practice.
elements.sent-2411-015: A large amount of articles are available on discretisation of Navier-Stokes equations.
elements.sent-2411-016: First, the problems without the heat equation have been studied extensively in different works; see, for example, \cite{EYMARD-1,EYMARD2,A3,A4 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recently, several studies have started analysing stationary coupled Navier-Stokes and heat equations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Without exhaustivity, we cite that problems are discretised by a spectral method in \cite{A8 and a finite element method in \cite{A9 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Using the GDM, \cite{Y-NSP-2021 provides a complete convergence analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In \cite{REF4 , virtual element discretisation is employed to obtain an order of convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Although several works on the numerical approximation of unsteady Navier-Stokes coupled with heat equations have been carried out in \cite{REF1,A10,A11 - to our knowledge- there is no available literature on the numerical analysis of non-conforming discretisation of the model \eqref{nsp .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: This paper aims to utilise the gradient discretisation method to propose a generic scheme and a new convergence analysis approach for the model \eqref{nsp .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The rest of the paper is organised as follows: In Section \ref{sec-2 , we state our assumptions and define the continuous weak formulation of our model.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Also, we introduce the fully discrete scheme and some properties.
elements.sent-2411-025: In Section \ref{sec-3 , we prove the proposed method's convergence in space and time variables under the physical hypothesis on data.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Section \ref{sec-numerical is devoted to numerical experiments to justify the theoretical results.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Let $\O$ be a connected open subset of $\RR^d\; (d=1,2,3)$ with a boundary $\dr\O$.
elements.sent-2411-028: We use the notation $\L^2(\O)^d$ for the space of square-integrable vector functions and $\H^1(\O)^d$ for the Sobolev space of vector functions.
elements.sent-2411-029: For $T>0$, we denote by $L^2(0,T;\H^1(\O)^d)$, the space of vector functions $\v$ such that for almost all $t\in[0,T]$, $\v(\cdot,t) \in \H^1(\O)$, and it is endowed with norm defined by \begin{equation}\|\v\|_{\L^2(0,T;\H^1(\O)^d)}=\Big(\dsp\int_0^T\|\v\|_{\H^1(\O)^d} \ud t\Big)^{1/2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-030: For scalar functions, we use the notations $L^2(\O)$ and $L^2(0,T;H^1(\O))$.
elements.sent-2411-031: From now on, let$''\cdot''$ be the dot product on $\RR^d$, if $\bvarphi=(\varphi_{ij )_{i,j=1,...,d}$ and $\bpsi=(\psi_{ij )_{i,j=1,...,d \in \RR^{d \times d}$, $\bvarphi:\bpsi=\sum_{i,j=1 ^d \varphi_{ij}\psi_{ij}$ is the doubly contracted product on $\RR^{d \times d}$, and \begin{equation}\label{eq-A} \cA(\u,\v,\w)=\dsp\sum_{i,j=1}^d\dsp\int_\O u_i(\dr_i v_j)w_j \ud \x, \quad \forall (\u,\v,\w) \in \H_0^1(\O)^d \times \H_0^1(\O)^d \times \H_0^1(\O)^d,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq-B} \cB(\u,r,S)=\dsp\sum_{i=1}\dsp\int_\O u_i(\dr_i r)S \ud \x, \quad \forall (\u,r,S) \in \H_0^1(\O)^d \times H_0^1(\O) \times H_0^1(\O).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We assume the model data satisfies the following conditions: \begin{equation}\label{assump-nsp} \left. \begin{aligned} \mu is a positive constant, $V \in L^\infty(\RR)$, and it holds, for two positive constants $a_1$ and $a_2$, for $\xi \in \RR$, $a_1 \leq V(\xi) \leq a_2$, \g \in L^2(0,T;\L^2(\O)^d) and h \in L^2(0,T;L^2(\O)), \u_0 \in \H_0^1(\O)^d and S_0 \in H_0^1(\O). \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{assump-nsp} \left.  \mu is a positive constant, $V \in L^\infty(\RR)$, and it holds, for two positive constants $a_1$ and $a_2$, for $\xi \in \RR$, $a_1 \leq V(\xi) \leq a_2$, \g \in L^2(0,T;\L^2(\O)^d) and h \in L^2(0,T;L^2(\O)), \u_0 \in \H_0^1(\O)^d and S_0 \in H_0^1(\O).  \right.\end{equation}

Under the above assumptions, the weak solution to \eqref{nsp is to find $( ar\u, ar p, ar S) \in L^2(0,T;\H_0^1(\O)^d) 	imes L^2(0,T;L_0^2(\O)) 	imes L^2(0,T;H_0^1(\O))$, such that,  egin{subequations  egin{equation}\label{nsp-weak-1}  egin{aligned} for all $\v \in L^2(0,T;\H_0^1(\O)^d)$ such that $\dr_t \v \in L^2(\O	imes (0,T))$ and $\v(\cdot,T)=0$ -\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O  ar\u(\x,t)\dr_t \v \ud \x \ud t -\dsp\int_\O \u_0 \v(\x,0) \ud \x +\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O V( ar S)(\x,t)
abla ar \u(\x,t) : 
abla\v(\x) \ud \x \ud t +\dsp\int_0^T\cA( ar\u(\x,t), ar\u(\x,t),\v(\x)) \ud t -\dsp\int_\O(\div \v)(\x)  ar p(\x,t) \ud \x =\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O \g(\x,t) \v(\x) \ud \x \ud t, \end{aligned}\end{equation}  egin{equation}\label{nsp-weak-2} for all q \in L^2(0,T;L_0^2(\O)), \quad -\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O \div  ar\u(\x,t) q(\x) \ud \x \ud t =0,\end{equation}  egin{equation}\label{nsp-weak-3}  egin{aligned} for all $r \in L^2(0,T;H_0^1(\O))$ such that $\dr_t r \in L^2(\O	imes (0,T))$ and $r(\cdot,T)=0$ \quad-\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O  ar S(\x,t)\dr_t r(\x) \ud \x \ud t -\dsp\int_\O S_0 r(\x,0) \ud \x \quad+\mu \dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O 
abla ar S(\x,t)\cdot 
abla r(\x) \ud \x \ud t +\dsp\int_0^T\cB( ar\u(\x,t), ar S(\x,t),r(\x)) \ud t =\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O h(\x,t)r(\x) \ud \x \ud t, \end{aligned}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $L_0^2(\O):=\{ q\in L^2(\O)\;:\; \dsp\int_\O q(\x) \ud \x=0 \}$, and $\cA$ and $\cB$ are respectively defined by \eqref{eq-A and \eqref{eq-B .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{nsp-weak-1}  for all $\v \in L^2(0,T;\H_0^1(\O)^d)$ such that $\dr_t \v \in L^2(\O\times (0,T))$ and $\v(\cdot,T)=0$ -\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O \bar\u(\x,t)\dr_t \v \ud \x \ud t -\dsp\int_\O \u_0 \v(\x,0) \ud \x +\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O V(\bar S)(\x,t)\nabla\bar \u(\x,t) : \nabla\v(\x) \ud \x \ud t +\dsp\int_0^T\cA(\bar\u(\x,t),\bar\u(\x,t),\v(\x)) \ud t -\dsp\int_\O(\div \v)(\x) \bar p(\x,t) \ud \x =\dsp\int_0^T\dsp\int_\O \g(\x,t) \v(\x) \ud \x \ud t, \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-035: Under the above standard assumptions on data, \cite{REF1 shows that there exists at least one solution to the problem \eqref{nsp-weak .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Now, let us construct the gradient discretisation method for our model.
elements.sent-2411-037: For the sake of completeness, we reintroduce the discrete elements (defined in \cite[Definition 2.2]{Y-NSP-2021 ) to discretise the spatial space $\O$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: A gradient discretisation $\disc$ for the Navier--Stokes model coupled with the heat equation is given by $\disc=(\X_{\disc,0 ,Y_\disc, \Y_{\disc,0 , \Pi_\disc, \widetilde\Pi_\disc, \nabla_\disc, \widetilde\nabla_\disc, \div_\disc, \chi_\disc, \cA_\disc, \cB_\disc)$, where:.}}

elements.sent-2411-044: At any time step $(n + 1)$, we note that a gradient scheme for a stationary problem is solved.
elements.sent-2411-045: Applying the same reasoning as in \cite[Section 2]{A8 , we can easily derive the existence of a solution to the elliptic discrete problem due to the finite dimension of discrete spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Below, we introduce some properties of the space-time gradient discretisation to guarantee the convergence of corresponding gradient schemes.
elements.sent-2411-047: These properties are slight adaptations of the ones stated in \cite{EYMARD-1 to deal with the new discrete elements introduced in our gradient discretisation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: A sequence of a space-time gradient discretisation $(\disc_{T_m )_{m\in\NN}$ is said to be.}
elements.section-2411-003: Discrete Energy Estimates And Main Results
elements.sent-2411-049: First, we derive some estimates on discrete solutions in order to establish our main results.

elements.sent-2411-073: In this section, we perform the hybrid finite volume methods to solve the Navier-Stokes problem \eqref{nsp on the square domain $\O=[-1,1]^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The scheme is a kind of polytopal method that preserves the physical properties of models.
elements.sent-2411-075: With a specific choice of the discrete elements given in Definition \ref{def-gd-nsp-T , we can show that the method can be presented in the gradient scheme format (the scheme \eqref{nsp-gs ), we refer the reader to \cite[Section 4]{Y-NSP-2021 for details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: We use two different types of polygonal meshes to partition the spatial domain, as displayed in Figure \ref{fig-mesh .}
elements.sent-2411-077: A backward Euler discretisation is employed for the time step, and the test is performed at $T=0.1$.
elements.figure-2411-001: \label{fig-mesh} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{cc} \includegraphics[width=0.40\linewidth]{mesh-1.png} \includegraphics[width=0.40\linewidth]{mesh-2.png} Triangular Distroted \end{tabular} \end{center} \caption{Sample of the polygonal meshes.} \label{fig-1}
elements.sent-2411-078: In order to investigate the quality of the hybrid finite volume methods, we measure the errors as the difference between the exact solution and the discrete one in a suitable norm.
elements.sent-2411-079: More precisely, we use the following quantities: \begin{equation}\label{eq-error} E_1:=\frac{\| \bar \u(\cdot) - \Pi_\disc \u\|_{\L^{2}(\O)}}{\| \bar \u(\cdot)\|_{\L^{2}(\O)}},\; E_2:=\frac{\| \bar p(\cdot) - \chi_\disc p\|_{L^{2}(\O)}}{\| \bar p(\cdot)\|_{L^{2}(\O)}},\; and E_3:=\frac{\| \bar S(\cdot) - \widetilde{\Pi}_\disc S\|_{L^{2}(\O)}}{\| \bar S(\cdot)\|_{L^{2}(\O)}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}\label{eq-error} E_1:=\frac{\| \bar \u(\cdot) - \Pi_\disc \u\|_{\L^{2}(\O)}}{\| \bar \u(\cdot)\|_{\L^{2}(\O)}},\; E_2:=\frac{\| \bar p(\cdot) - \chi_\disc p\|_{L^{2}(\O)}}{\| \bar p(\cdot)\|_{L^{2}(\O)}},\; and E_3:=\frac{\| \bar S(\cdot) - \widetilde{\Pi}_\disc S\|_{L^{2}(\O)}}{\| \bar S(\cdot)\|_{L^{2}(\O)}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: First, we perform the test on the triangular mesh type to examine the accuracy of the scheme where the Navier-Stokes equations are not dependent on the temperature ($V(S)=1$).
elements.sent-2411-081: The exact solution is \begin{equation}\label{exact-u} \bar\u(x,y):= \begin{pmatrix} \bar u_1 \bar u_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sin((\pi + t)y) \cos((\pi + t)x) \cos((\pi + t)y) \sin((\pi + t)x) \end{pmatrix},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{exact-p} \bar p(x, y) = \sin((\pi + t)x) \cos((\pi + t)y),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{exact-S} \bar S(x, y) = t \sin(x + y),\end{equation} We post in Figure \ref{fig-1 and Figure \ref{fig-2 the convergence rate graphs of the velocity, pressure, and temperature errors.}}
elements.formula-2411-037: \begin{equation}\label{exact-u} \bar\u(x,y):=  \bar u_1 \bar u_2  =  \sin((\pi + t)y) \cos((\pi + t)x) \cos((\pi + t)y) \sin((\pi + t)x) ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: The convergence rates are close to one, which is acceptable compared to well-known $\mathcal O(h)$-estimates.
elements.sent-2411-083: Furthermore, we make use of the least squares residual test.
elements.sent-2411-084: The resultant errors can be presented in the following formula: \begin{equation}E=Ch^r,\end{equation} where $C$ and $r$ are positive constants, $h$ is the mesh size, and $E$ stands to errors computed by \eqref{eq-error .}
elements.sent-2411-086: We observe that the slopes are very close to one with the least squares residual of $0.21$, $0.20$, $0.11$, and $0.08$ with respect to errors on $\bar \u=(\bar u_1,\bar u_2)$, $\bar p$ and $\bar S$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-087: Second, one vital feature of the hybrid finite volume method (used here) is the flexibility in dealing with complex geometries distorting cells.
